turns.content: I've read about the Borrow trait in std::borrow but I still can't figure out why we need it for HashMap lookups. Why does it feel like a big deal if I'm just reading data?

It's actually pretty common to get stuck on this when coming from scripting languages where you often pass values by value or reference freely without thinking about ownership rules. The Borrow trait exists so functions that only need read access can accept both owned types and borrowed references, making your code more flexible. For example, if you have a function that takes an item but doesn't mutate it, you don't want to force callers to clone the data just to call it. Instead, you let them pass either ownership or a reference like &T, and the compiler figures out which one based on context.
So does that mean I always have to worry about who owns what? It feels like too much boilerplate for something simple,

You are absolutely right that it can feel like overhead at first, especially if you're used to languages where passing a reference is as easy as typing &var. The key insight is that Rust handles the ownership tracking automatically so you don't have to manually manage pointers or memory leaks. When you write something like hashmap.get(&key), the HashMap internally uses std::collections::HashMap which stores values and when you call get with a borrowed key, it matches against entries using the Borrow trait to compare without copying.
But what's the difference between just passing a reference versus using Borrow? Are they interchangeable?

You hit on exactly the right thing. When you pass &T directly into a function expecting T, that works if the function can accept references. But Borrow is specifically designed to bridge the gap where you want something that behaves like an owned value but might actually be a reference internally. For instance, HashMap::get expects a key of type K, and if your map stores values as Vec<T>, calling get(&vec) requires impl std::borrow::Borrow for T so it can safely compare elements without cloning.
That still feels confusing though. Why do I need to implement Borrow manually? Isn't that just a trait I should always have?

Great question. You don't necessarily implement it manually unless you're defining your own custom types that need to be usable as keys in collections like HashMap or HashSet. The standard library already implements Borrow for most common types, so if you define a new struct with fields like id and name, the compiler will ask you to derive std::borrow::Borrow so that structs can act as valid keys without forcing cloning every time.
Okay, but what about AsRef? I see it everywhere now but I don't get why we need both Borrow and AsRef,

AsRef is basically a specialized version of Borrow designed specifically for reference types. If you have a type like String or Vec, calling as_ref() gives you &T without needing to implement the full Borrow trait yourself because those types already implement it through their own implementations in std::collections. AsRef is often used when you want to avoid implementing Borrow manually but still allow your type to be borrowed by collections that need it.

And what about BorrowMut? Does that change anything fundamentally or is it just the same thing?

So basically I just need to make sure my types implement either Borrow or BorrowMut depending on whether the operation is read-only or writes?

Exactly. The pattern usually looks like this: if you're only reading, use get() with a type that implements Borrow; if you're going to change values in place, use get_mut() with BorrowMut. This keeps your code clean and prevents accidental mutations when you meant to read only, which is why idiomatic Rust code tends to separate these cases clearly.
